metadata.summary: Serverless computing fundamentals: key characteristics, FaaS providers, serverless databases, supporting services, and fit-assessment for architects.

content.title: Serverless Architecture — Foundations
content.summary: Serverless computing abstracts infrastructure management, allowing developers to focus on code. Functions scale from zero to thousands of instances automatically, are billed per execution (millisecond granularity), and are stateless by design — all state lives in external storage.

why.p: Serverless eliminates server provisioning, patching, and capacity planning. The pay-per-execution model means you pay only for actual compute time, making it extremely cost-efficient for variable or spiky workloads. Auto-scaling handles traffic bursts without operator intervention.

list.item: REST/GraphQL APIs with auto-scaling, pay-per-request, and easy deployment needs.
Event processing with native integration with queues, streams, and storage.
Webhooks handling spiky, unpredictable traffic.
Scheduled jobs (cron) with no idle costs between executions.
Image/video processing requiring horizontal scale for parallel processing.
Data transformations: ETL pipelines, real-time processing.
MVPs and prototypes needing fast iteration with minimal ops overhead.
Chatbots and AI workloads with burst traffic patterns and API integrations.
IoT backends handling millions of device events.
Long-running tasks (>15 min) — Lambda timeout limit; use ECS/Fargate or Step Functions instead.
Consistent high load — cost-inefficient at scale; containers or EC2 are cheaper.
Low latency requirements (<50ms) — cold starts add latency; use containers or keep-warm strategies.
Complex stateful applications — requires external state management; consider Kubernetes or traditional servers.
GPU/specialized hardware — limited instance types; use EC2 with GPU or SageMaker.
WebSockets (persistent connections) — use API Gateway WebSocket or AppSync instead.
Heavy compute (video encoding) — memory/CPU limits and cost; use EC2 Spot or dedicated instances.
Compliance requiring physical isolation — multi-tenancy concerns; use dedicated infrastructure.

section.name: faas-providers
section.p: Key FaaS (Functions as a Service) platforms and their constraints as of 2025:

list.item: AWS Lambda: 15 min max timeout, 10 GB max memory, supports Node, Python, Go, Java, .NET, Ruby, Rust.
Azure Functions: unlimited timeout (Durable Functions can run indefinitely), 14 GB max memory, supports Node, Python, Java, .NET, PowerShell.
GCP Cloud Functions (2nd gen): 60 min max timeout, 32 GB max memory, supports Node, Python, Go, Java, .NET, Ruby, PHP.
Cloudflare Workers: 30s CPU time, 128 MB max memory, supports JS/TS, Rust, WASM.
Vercel Functions (Pro): 300s max timeout, 3 GB max memory, supports Node, Python, Go, Ruby.
Netlify Functions: 26s sync timeout, 1 GB max memory, supports Node and Go.

section.name: serverless-databases
section.p: Serverless-compatible databases that scale to zero or on demand:

list.item: DynamoDB (NoSQL key-value): high-scale APIs, AWS-native; pay-per-request or provisioned.
PlanetScale (MySQL/Vitess): web apps, schema branching; billed per rows read/written.
Neon (PostgreSQL): full SQL with branching; billed on compute time.
Supabase (PostgreSQL): full-stack, real-time; billed on compute + storage.
Upstash (Redis): caching, rate limiting; billed per requests.
Turso (SQLite/libSQL): edge and embedded use cases; billed per rows read.
CockroachDB Serverless (Distributed SQL): global distribution; billed per request units.

section.name: supporting-services
section.p: Cloud-provider supporting services for serverless architectures (AWS / Azure / GCP):

list.item: API Gateway: AWS API Gateway / AppSync, Azure API Management, GCP Cloud Endpoints / Apigee.
Event Bus: AWS EventBridge, Azure Event Grid, GCP Eventarc.
Message Queue: AWS SQS, Azure Service Bus, GCP Cloud Tasks.
Pub/Sub: AWS SNS, Azure Service Bus Topics, GCP Pub/Sub.
Workflow: AWS Step Functions, Azure Durable Functions, GCP Workflows.
Object Storage: AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, GCP Cloud Storage.
Auth: AWS Cognito, Azure AD B2C, Firebase Auth.
Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager.
Variable/unpredictable traffic? → Serverless is ideal; containers may be cheaper for steady load.
Execution time < 15 minutes? → Lambda is viable; otherwise use Fargate/ECS.
Latency spikes acceptable? → Standard Lambda; use provisioned concurrency or containers if not.
Event-driven processing? → Serverless is ideal; consider always-on services for non-event workloads.
Rapid iteration needed? → Fast deployments; works for any approach.

section.name: agent-prompts
section.p: LLM prompt template for initial serverless fit assessment:
section.prompt:
Evaluate whether serverless is appropriate for this use case:
Application: [description] Workload characteristics: - Request rate: [X/second average, X/second peak] - Request duration: [X ms average] - Traffic pattern: [spiky/steady/predictable] - State requirements: [stateless/stateful/sessions]
Current infrastructure: [if migrating] Team experience: [serverless experience level]
Please analyze: 1. Serverless suitability score (1-10) with reasoning 2. Key risks and mitigations 3. Alternative approaches if serverless is not ideal 4. Migration path if applicable 5. Cost comparison (serverless vs containers vs VMs)

I need to design a serverless architecture for [APPLICATION TYPE].
Requirements: - Expected traffic: [X requests/day, peak patterns] - Latency requirements: [p95 < Xms] - Data model: [describe main entities] - Integrations: [external APIs, services] - Compliance: [GDPR, HIPAA, etc.] - Budget constraints: [if any]
Current tech stack: [existing technologies] Cloud provider preference: [AWS/Azure/GCP/multi-cloud]
Please recommend: 1. Overall architecture pattern (event-driven, API-centric, etc.) 2. Specific services to use 3. Data storage strategy 4. Key design decisions with tradeoffs 5. Cost estimate at expected scale

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: When to use this methodology with agents:

list.item: Designing event-driven backends (HTTP APIs, queue processors, scheduled jobs) that need pay-per-execution and auto-scale-to-zero economics.
Ad-hoc cost-modeling: Lambda cost vs Fargate cost crossover, GB-seconds estimation, Provisioned Concurrency ROI.
Agents hallucinate AWS quotas, runtime versions, region availability, and pricing — these change quarterly. Always verify with aws lambda get-account-settings or pricing API.
Cold-start estimates are runtime/memory/dependency dependent — LLM cannot benchmark, only suggest.

checklist.check: Analyze traffic pattern: identify peak load, variability, and time-of-day/seasonal patterns.
Define latency requirements: acceptable p50/p95/p99 and cold start tolerance.
Document execution characteristics: max expected time, memory requirements, CPU vs I/O bound.
Define cost model: budget constraints, cost at expected and 10x scale, comparison with containers/VMs.
Verify compliance requirements: data residency, encryption, audit logging, multi-tenancy isolation.
Assess serverless fit using the decision matrix above.
